div.h1: x86 vs ARM Memory Model Comparison

p.strong: Allowed Reorderings in Concurrent Memory Access

div.p: Strong Memory Model Most reorderings prevented by hardware. Only store→load reordering allowed.
Implication: Code that "works" on x86 may break on ARM without explicit barriers. Store buffer causes store→load reordering (typically 50-100 cycle window).
Weak Memory Model Hardware allows most reorderings for performance. Explicit barriers required.
Implication: Any relaxed code that "works" on x86 is likely BROKEN on ARM! Always use acquire/release semantics for portable concurrent code.

The Bug That Hides on x86 c // Thread 1 (Producer) data = 42; // (1) Store data ready = 1; // (2) Store flag // Thread 2 (Consumer)
while (!ready) {} // (3) Load flag print(data); // (4) Load data
Release ensures all prior writes visible before the store. Acquire ensures all subsequent reads see data after the load. Works correctly on both x86 and ARM!
Usually Works (by accident) Store→Store prevented: (1) before (2) Load→Load prevented: (3) before (4) But: Store→Load CAN reorder! Rare edge cases may still fail.
BROKEN Without Barriers! All reorderings possible:

ul.li: (4) may execute before (3) Consumer may see ready=1 but data=0 (uninitialized)!

div.p: CPU may execute load before store completes (store buffer). Only reordering allowed on x86!
